exemplar.title:
To what extent does Body Condition Score (1-9) and body mass (kg) impact resting and 
active heart rate (beats per minute) in purebred canines (Canis lupus familiaris)?

detailedExemplarData.title:
To what extent does Body Condition Score (1-9) and body mass (kg) impact resting and 
active heart rate (beats per minute) in purebred canines (Canis lupus familiaris)?

subject.title: Biology
subject.description:
**IB Biology** is a **Group 4** subject in the **International Baccalaureate Diploma Programme (IBDP)**. It explores **four interconnected themes: Unity and Diversity, Form and Function, Interaction and Interdependence, and Continuity and Change**, with a strong focus on **experimental work, scientific inquiry, data analysis, and practical investigation skills**.

The course is available at **Standard Level (SL, 150 teaching hours)** and **Higher Level (HL, 240 teaching hours)**, with **first assessment in 2025**.

### Assessment at a Glance

| Assessment | Format | SL | HL | Weighting |
|------------|--------|----|----|-----------|
| **Paper 1** | Multiple-choice and data-based questions | 1 hour 20 min | 2 hours | 36% |
| **Paper 2** | Short and extended response questions | 1 hour 30 min | 2 hours 30 min | 44% |
| **Scientific Investigation** | Individual research project | 10 hours | 10 hours | 20% |

strengths: Research question is exceptionally focused and subject-specific, clearly stating variables and population
Methodology is varied and well-justified, with detailed procedures enabling replication
Topic is communicated effectively with comprehensive biological context and ethical considerations
weaknesses: Controlled variables table omits full descriptions (e.g. âHealthâ) reducing reproducibility
Some methodological rationale (BMI calculation) is misplaced outside the methods section
Details on timing for rest intervals and trotting speed calibration are lacking
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The submission meets almost all descriptors of the top band for Focus and Method. The research question is sharply defined (band-6), and methodology is appropriate and detailed (band-5). Minor gaps in procedural detail and organisation prevent the absolute maximum, placing the work solidly at 5 out of 6.

strengths: Terminology and biological concepts are used accurately and consistently throughout
Source material is applied in relevant ways to explain key physiological mechanisms
weaknesses: Use of sources remains largely descriptive; key studies are quoted rather than critically interrogated
Citation formatting is inconsistent and occasional data tables lack clear labels
Some factual statements (e.g. tissue-specific heart-rate effects) lack supporting references
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The work demonstrates clear subject knowledge and accurate use of terminology (band-5) but only partially effective application of source material, with limited analytical engagement (band-4). On balance, the investigation aligns with the descriptor for 4 out of 6.

strengths: Substantial quantitative analysis with means, standard deviations and correlation coefficients
Logical subgroup stratification and interpretation linked to physiological theory
Coherent argument structure, with results consistently tied back to the research question
weaknesses: No inferential statistics (e.g. p-values, confidence intervals) to support conclusions
Evaluation of limitations is present but remains cursory and descriptive
Error analysis of shorter heart-rate sampling intervals is suggested but not fully developed
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: The investigation delivers good to very good analytical work that remains consistently focused on the research question (band-7â9) and provides solid evaluation and argumentation. The absence of inferential testing and deeper evaluation constrains it to 8 out of 12.

strengths: All required layout and structural elements are present and correctly applied
Tables and figures are captioned and referenced, supporting readability
Consistent pagination, title placement, and numbering enhance navigation
weaknesses: Several figures and tables are mis-labelled or split awkwardly across pages
No abstract is provided to summarise purpose, methods, results and conclusions
Mixed citation styles and HTML math tags detract from professional polish
feedback.calculationMethod: Holistic AI evaluation: Presentation meets the highest descriptors for clear structure and formatting (band-3â4). Minor inconsistencies in labelling and lack of an abstract prevent absolute perfection, justifying the top mark of 4 out of 4.

strandSelections.reasoning:
The essay **follows the prescribed EE structure faithfully**: title page, abstract substitute (word count), table of contents, introduction, background, methodology, data, discussion, conclusion and bibliography. Sub-sections, numbered tables and labelled figures aid navigation, and the flow of information is logical. 

Although some large tables are split awkwardly across pages, the ordering of material is never confusing and each sectionâs purpose is clear. The organisation therefore meets the descriptor of a clear and appropriate structure throughout. 

Minor formatting issues do not undermine structural coherence, so the highest band (3-4) is appropriate; a score of 4 is awarded.

All required layout elements are present: consistent page numbering, readable font, double spacing, correctly placed word count and title, and a functional table of contents with page references. Tables and figures are generally captioned and referenced within the text. 

Some imperfections persistâlow-resolution images, occasional misaligned columns, and duplicated word-count entriesâbut these do not impede comprehension. Citation style, while not fully consistent, includes all necessary information and a comprehensive bibliography. 

Given that most formatting conventions are correctly applied and presentation supports readability, the work fits the 3-4 band; a score of 4 is therefore appropriate.

The essay presents a **coherent argumentative line**: introduction â background â methodology â multi-layered results â discussion â conclusion. Claims about obesityâs impact on heart rate are consistently supported by data tables and figures, and counter-points (e.g., breed variability) are addressed. 

Transitions between sections are generally smooth, and each point builds logically on the previous one. Minor narrative inconsistencies (e.g., occasional abrupt sentence starts, brief tangential comments) disrupt flow slightly, and some conclusions could be more tightly linked to specific data values. 

Given the overall clarity with a few lapses, the work aligns with the 7-9 band but does not attain seamless sophistication, so a conservative score of 7 is assigned.

The student introduces the problem of canine obesity clearly, provides solid biological context (e.g., adipose tissue function, cardiovascular responses) and defines key constructs such as BCS and body mass with appropriate detail. 

They integrate concise explanations of physiological mechanisms (sympathetic activation, vascular resistance) that justify the studyâs importance and link directly to the dependent variable (heart-rate). Background information is largely correct and sufficiently detailed for a Biology EE.  

Minor weaknessesâoccasional repetition, some awkward phrasing and an over-long table of contentsâslightly reduce overall polish, but the topic remains **accurately and effectively communicated with comprehensive explanation**, fitting the 5-6 band; a score of 5 is therefore warranted.

The investigation draws on a **wide range of recent veterinary and physiological sources** to establish background and support discussion. Citations are generally relevant and integrated to explain concepts such as adipose physiology, metabolic scaling and breed-specific heart-rate norms. 

Nevertheless, source use remains **largely explanatory rather than analytical**; primary research findings are quoted rather than critically contrasted with the studentâs own data, and key literature is seldom interrogated for methodology or limitations. Some references (web articles, popular sources) are of uneven academic rigour, and citation formatting is inconsistent. 

Overall, the work demonstrates mostly relevant application and clear subject knowledge but only partial critical engagement with sources, aligning best with the 3-4 band; a top-of-band score of 4 is appropriate.

The student conducts **substantial quantitative analysis**: means, standard deviations and correlation coefficients are calculated for overall data and for breed-size subgroups, supported by numerous graphs with trend lines. The analysis remains focused on the research question and identifies patterns such as stronger resting-HR relationships in small and large breeds. 

Strengths include large sample size (n = 109), subgroup stratification and comparison of two obesity proxies (BCS vs relative BMI). The analysis is logically sequenced and interpretations link back to physiological mechanisms. 

However, the absence of inferential statistical tests (e.g., p-values, confidence intervals) and limited exploration of variability moderate the depth of insight, positioning the work comfortably in the 7-9 band; a mid-band score of 8 is appropriate.

The student employs an **appropriate and varied methodology**: a 9-point BCS assessment, breed-specific relative BMI calculations, triplicate stethoscope readings of heart rate, and correlation statistics. Ethical, controlled and independent variables are enumerated in detail, and the rationale for instrument choice (stethoscope vs electronic monitor) is well argued. 

Controls for sex, breed, age, reproductive status and environment are thoughtfully justified, indicating an awareness of confounding factors. Materials, sample sizes and procedures are reported with sufficient clarity to allow replication. 

However, concerns remain about heart-rate reliability over the short 20-s count and limited discussion of statistical significance tests. These minor gaps leave the work just short of full marks, meriting a solid 5 within the 5-6 band.

The discussion **interprets correlations, compares subgroup trends, and acknowledges methodological limitations** (short heart-rate sampling, small giant-breed sample). The student links findings to physiological concepts (vascular resistance, oxygen demand) and to published studies on weight loss and cardiac workload, demonstrating sound evaluative thinking. 

Yet evaluation of limitations remains brief and largely descriptive; proposals for improving reliability (e.g., longer HR measurement, inclusion of statistical significance testing) are mentioned but not developed in detail. Consideration of alternative explanations (stress, fitness level) is noted but not deeply analysed. 

Overall, the strand shows good but not exhaustive critical evaluation, matching the middle 4-6 band; a score of 5 reflects this performance.

The research questionââTo what extent does Body Condition Score (1-9) and body mass (kg) impact resting and active heart rate (beats per minute) in purebred canines (Canis lupus familiaris)?ââis **explicitly stated, sharply delimited and entirely appropriate for Biology**. 

It narrows the investigation to two defined independent variables, two specific dependent variables, and a clearly identified population (purebred adult dogs). This framing allows for measurable, quantitative analysis and aligns with relevant biological concepts of cardiovascular physiology and obesity.

Because the question is unambiguous, focused and solidly grounded in subject-specific science, it satisfies all descriptors of the highest band; the maximum strand mark of 6 is justified.

Throughout the essay the student employs **biological and veterinary terminology accurately and consistently**âe.g., sympathetic activation, vascular resistance, adiposity, correlation coefficient, relative BMI, standard deviation. Definitions of BCS and methodological terms are clear, and statistical vocabulary is used correctly. 

Concepts such as metabolic scaling, cardiovascular workload and endocrine roles of adipose tissue are applied appropriately to interpret results. Occasional phrasing lapses do not impede comprehension and do not reflect conceptual misunderstanding. 

Given the overall precision and consistency, but with sporadic stylistic repetitions, the work meets the descriptors for the 5-6 band; a score of 5 has been awarded.

annotations.comment: Figures 5â7 do not annotate correlation coefficients or confidence bands on the plots. Adding r (or RÂ²) directly on each graph would improve interpretive clarity.
Table 8âs standard deviations of r values (0.10 and 0.20) suggest high variability. Discuss potential causes (sample size, measurement error) to contextualize these fluctuations.
Repetitive use of terms such as âheart rateâ and âcorrelationâ in the discussion creates monotony. Vary sentence structure and vocabulary to enhance readability.
Figure 2 is titled as âresting HRâ but the image and description refer to active pulse data. Ensure figure titles, images and captions align.
The table of contents spans significant page space and repeats entries, which hinders quick navigation. Streamline headings and remove duplicate items to improve readability.
The repeated âWord count: 3968â entry in the contents is unnecessary. Ensure only a single wordâcount entry is included at the beginning as per formatting guidelines.
The 'Health' controlledâvariable row is incomplete. All controlled variables should have full descriptions and control methods to ensure reproducibility.
Measuring heart rate over 20âs introduces a Â±5% potential error. Consider fullâminute counts or automated monitors for improved reliability, and discuss implications for data precision.
The rationale for calculating relative BMI appears here rather than in methodology. Relocate the formula and justification to methods for coherence and replicability.
The word count appears twice (pages 1 and 2). Remove the redundant entry to comply with formatting requirements and avoid confusion.
The description of stethoscope measurement Â±3 bpm lacks detail on calibration or observer training. Include rationale for error margin and procedures to standardize manual counts.
Math formatting still uses HTML <math> tags. Convert to consistent LaTeX notation (e.g. $$\pm 3\,\text{bpm}$$) to align with academic standards.
Table 1 is split awkwardly across pages, and key entries (e.g. Health) are truncated. Consolidate the table or break into manageable subâtables to maintain clarity.
The discussion of muscle versus fat effects on heart rate lacks citation. Reference veterinary physiology sources when distinguishing these tissueâspecific impacts.

The report omits an abstract summarizing the investigationâs purpose, methods, results and conclusion. Including a concise abstract (max 300 words) would clarify the studyâs scope and key findings, enhancing reader orientation.
The phrase â59% percentâ is redundant. Use â59%â or â59 percentâ only once to maintain precise and accurate terminology.
Figure 1 is actually a table of BCS categories but is labelled as a figure. Use consistent labeling (âTable 1â) and cross-reference appropriately.
The opening statistic (â59% overweightâ) is presented without citation. Provide a reference to the original study or database to support this prevalence figure.
Table 3 presents raw measurements for 109 subjects, which is overwhelming. Summarize key statistics in the text, referencing the table only for detailed values.
The text mentions calculating means and SDs but directs readers to later tables. Introduce summary tables immediately after discussion to maintain logical flow.
The materials list uses bullet symbols inconsistently. Present key equipment in a numbered or tabular format to improve uniformity and reader navigation.
The ethical considerations section thoroughly outlines nonâinvasive methods, owner consent, and welfare provisions, demonstrating strong adherence to animal care standards.
The procedure is detailed but omits precise timing for rest intervals and acclimation. Specify the exact settling period before each measurement to reduce variability.
âTable 3 continuedâ lacks context. Repeat column headers or include a brief caption on each continuation page to maintain interpretability.
Table 4 combines four breed groups into one dense grid. Splitting into separate tables per group would facilitate clearer comparison and reduce visual clutter.
Table 5 lacks clear column headers for âResting HR meanâ and âActive HR meanâ. Label each variable explicitly to prevent ambiguity in data interpretation.
The trotting speed is described only as âapproximately 6 km/h.â Provide a calibrated pace or mechanical measurement to ensure consistent exercise stimulus across subjects.
Correlation results are reported without p-values or confidence intervals. Include inferential statistics to assess whether observed relationships are statistically significant.
The hypotheses are stated, but the statistical test for evaluating them is not specified. Indicate which correlation test (e.g. Pearson) and alpha level (e.g. 0.05) will be used.
Table 6 lists relative BMI values but omits sample sizes for each group. Indicate n per group to contextualize variability and reliability.
The definition âvalues â¤ 1.0â uses HTML <math> notation. Convert to LaTeX ($$\le1.0$$) to maintain consistent formatting of mathematical expressions.
Averaging correlation coefficients directly can bias results; consider Fisherâs z transformation when computing means of r values to ensure statistical validity.
Figure 4 panels combine plot and description within the same frame. Separate the caption from the data table and include r values and sample sizes on the chart for clarity.
Equation 1 presents correlation formula but does not define symbols (cov, Ï). Add a brief explanation or legend for each symbol to support reader understanding.
Figure 1âs alt text is overly detailed, restating the entire table. Condense the caption to key trends and reserve data tables for the appendix.
You note 20-s versus 60-s measurement impacts but do not quantify how shorter intervals affect SD. Include a brief error analysis to strengthen this methodological evaluation.
Multiple webâbased and nonâpeerâreviewed sources appear in the bibliography. Critically assess and prioritize peerâreviewed literature to strengthen academic rigor.
Figure captions incorrectly refer to âlarge groupâ when showing mediumâgroup data. Correct group labels to match the data displayed.
Figures 8â9 use twoâpanel layouts but omit axis titles on individual panels. Label axes on each plot to ensure selfâcontained interpretability.
Figures 10â11 are not referenced in the preceding results text. Cite each figure explicitly when discussing groupâspecific correlations to guide the reader.
The conclusion restates results and literature but lacks critical reflection on study limitations and specific recommendations for future research directions.
References follow multiple citation styles (APA, MLA, numeric). Adopt a single, consistent referencing format (e.g. APA) throughout the bibliography.
